content.problemStatement:
The tennis tournament you organized has just finished and now you have to decide the ranking of the NNN players (indexed by 1,2,…,N1, 2, \dots, N1,2,…,N) who participated.
Each pair of players played exactly one game and for each game you know who won (there are no draws in tennis).

A ranking is a permutation P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2, \dots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​ of 1,2,…,N1, 2, \dots, N1,2,…,N; where P1P_1P1​ represents the winner of the tournament, P2P_2P2​ is the second in the ranking, etc.

A ranking is good if for any 1≤i<j≤min⁡(i+⌈N2⌉,N)1\le i\lt j\le \min(i+\lceil \frac N2\rceil, N)1≤i<j≤min(i+⌈2N​⌉,N) the player PiP_iPi​ won against PjP_jPj​.
In other words, a ranking is good if, for any two players whose ranking positions differ by at most ⌈N2⌉\lceil \frac N2\rceil⌈2N​⌉, the result of their game is consistent with their order in the ranking.

Find, if it exists, a good ranking.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT, the number of test cases. Then TTT test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN, the number of participants.
Then NNN lines follow. The iii-th of them contains a string SiS_iSi​ of length nnn whose characters are either 0 or 1. For any 1≤j≤N1\le j\le N1≤j≤N with j≠ij\neq ij=i, the jjj-th character of SiS_iSi​ is 0 if iii lost against jjj and 1 otherwise. It is guaranteed that for any i≠ji\neq ji=j the jjj-th character of SiS_iSi​ is different from the iii-th character of SjS_jSj​ (so that they are consistent). For any 1≤i≤N1\le i\le N1≤i≤N, the iii-th character of SiS_iSi​ is 0 (notice that this character does not correspond to any game in the tournament).


Output Format
For each test case, print −1-1−1 if a good ranking does not exist.


If a good ranking exists, print on a single line the nnn integers P1,P2,…,PNP_1,P_2,\dots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​ corresponding to a good ranking (P1P_1P1​ is the winning player, P2P_2P2​ is the runner-up, P3P_3P3​ is the third in the ranking, etc.).


If there is more than one good ranking, print any of them.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 1 0001\,0001000.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Explanation of the first testcase: There is only one participant and the only possible ranking is good.
Explanation of the second testcase: There are two participants and the participant 222 won against the participant 111. Thus the ranking 2,12, 12,1 (that is, player 222 wins the tournament) is good.
Explanation of the third testcase: One may check that there is not a good ranking. Let us explain why some possible rankings are not good:

The ranking P1=1,P2=2,P3=3P_1=1, P_2=2, P_3=3P1​=1,P2​=2,P3​=3 is not good because for i=1i=1i=1, j=3j=3j=3 (notice that 1≤i<j≤min⁡(N,i+⌈N2⌉)1\le i \lt j \le \min(N, i+\lceil \frac N2\rceil)1≤i<j≤min(N,i+⌈2N​⌉) since ⌈N2⌉=2\lceil \frac N2\rceil=2⌈2N​⌉=2) the result of the game between Pi=1P_i=1Pi​=1 and Pj=3P_j=3Pj​=3 is not consistent with their ranking positions (player 111 lost against player 333 but 111 has a better ranking position).
The ranking P1=3,P2=2,P3=1P_1=3, P_2=2, P_3=1P1​=3,P2​=2,P3​=1 is not good because for i=1i=1i=1, j=2j=2j=2 (notice that 1≤i<j≤min⁡(N,i+⌈N2⌉)1\le i \lt j \le \min(N, i+\lceil \frac N2\rceil)1≤i<j≤min(N,i+⌈2N​⌉) since ⌈N2⌉=2\lceil \frac N2\rceil=2⌈2N​⌉=2) the result of the game between Pi=3P_i=3Pi​=3 and Pj=2P_j=2Pj​=2 is not consistent with their ranking positions.
